question_text: Verify the address of the emergency. What is the exact address?
What is the callback number you are calling from?
Tell me exactly what happened.
How old is the patient?
Is s/he conscious?
Is s/he breathing normally?
Ask the patient to smile. Does one side of the face droop?
Ask the patient to raise both arms. Does one arm drift downward?
Ask the patient to repeat a simple sentence like 'The sky is blue.' Is the speech slurred or garbled?
What time did the symptoms start? When was s/he last known to be normal?
Did the symptoms come on suddenly?
Is there any numbness or weakness on one side of the body?
Is s/he confused or having trouble understanding what you're saying?
Can s/he see normally? Is there any vision loss or double vision?
Does s/he have a sudden severe headache — the worst headache of their life?
Is s/he dizzy or having trouble walking or keeping balance?
Has s/he vomited?
Has s/he had a seizure?
Does s/he have a history of stroke or TIA?
Does s/he have high blood pressure, diabetes, or atrial fibrillation?
Is s/he on blood thinners?
Has there been any recent head trauma?
Is there any neck pain or stiffness?
ECHO-level: Unconscious and not breathing with neurological symptoms
DELTA-level: Acute stroke signs (FAST positive) within 4.5 hours — time-critical
DELTA-level: Sudden worst headache of life with altered consciousness
DELTA-level: Acute unilateral weakness/numbness with speech difficulty
DELTA-level: Stroke symptoms with seizure
CHARLIE-level: FAST positive, onset > 4.5 hours or unknown, stable
condition: FAST positive AND onset > 4.5 hours AND stable

question_text: CHARLIE-level: Sudden severe headache with vomiting, no altered consciousness
condition: severe headache AND vomiting AND alert

question_text: CHARLIE-level: Resolving neurological symptoms — possible TIA
BRAVO-level: Mild facial droop only, no other deficits, onset unclear
condition: isolated facial droop AND onset unknown

question_text: BRAVO-level: Known TIA history with recurrent mild symptoms
ALPHA-level: Mild headache with numbness, fully resolved, no deficits on exam
OMEGA-level: Chronic stable neurological complaint, no acute changes, physician follow-up
condition: chronic stable AND no acute changes

question_text: Note the exact time the symptoms started — this is critical for treatment decisions.
Do NOT give the patient anything to eat or drink. Stroke can affect swallowing.
Keep the patient lying flat with the head slightly elevated. Do NOT sit them fully upright.
Do NOT give aspirin or any medication. The type of stroke must be determined first.
If the patient vomits, turn them on their side to keep the airway clear.
Keep the patient calm and still. Do not let them walk.
If the patient becomes unconscious and stops breathing, begin CPR.
Gather all medications — especially blood thinners — and have them ready for paramedics.
Stay on the line. Help is on the way. Time is critical.
If anything changes — new symptoms, loss of consciousness, or s/he stops breathing — call us back immediately.
